title: Case Study 1 — The Price of Certainty
subtitle: Pidilite Industries Ltd.

scenario:
## Background

Pidilite Industries Ltd. is India's dominant adhesives and construction chemicals company. Founded in 1959, it has spent six decades building a business that most investors struggle to value correctly — because it looks expensive on every standard metric, yet has compounded returns at ~18% CAGR for 20 years.

Its flagship product is **Fevicol**, a carpenter's adhesive so dominant that the brand name has become a verb in Indian languages. Carpenters don't ask for adhesive — they ask for Fevicol. This is not marketing hyperbole. It reflects a 70% market share in the organised adhesives segment that has held for three decades despite determined efforts by Henkel, 3M, and Sika to break it.

The company also owns M-Seal, Fevikwik, Dr. Fixit (waterproofing), and Roff (tile adhesives). Its products reach **4 million retail outlets** across India, a distribution network built over 40 years that no competitor has replicated.

## Financial Snapshot (FY2022–23, simplified)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Revenue | ₹12,780 crore |
| EBITDA | ₹2,100 crore (margin: 16.4%) |
| Net Profit (PAT) | ₹1,720 crore |
| Capital Expenditure | ₹320 crore |
| Free Cash Flow (approx) | ₹1,400 crore |
| Net Cash on Balance Sheet | ₹1,100 crore (zero debt) |
| Market Capitalisation | ~₹1,10,000 crore |
| Price-to-Earnings | ~64× |
| Price-to-Free-Cash-Flow | ~79× |
| 15-Year Revenue CAGR | ~13% |
| 15-Year PAT CAGR | ~15% |
| Avg. Return on Equity (15 yr) | ~27% |

## Why the Business Works

The economics are unusual. Fevicol is used in carpentry, construction, and packaging. It is a **consumable** — used once and gone, which means repeat purchase is structural, not optional. Yet it requires almost no capital to produce at scale: the factories are simple, inventory turns fast, and the customer does not require credit terms.

This combination — consumable product, brand loyalty, low capital intensity — means Pidilite converts roughly **₹8 out of every ₹10 of net profit into free cash flow**. Most manufacturing businesses convert 4–5. The company has no need for external capital; it funds all growth internally and still accumulates cash.

The moat is layered. First, brand trust built over 40 years with carpenters who train apprentices in its use. Second, distribution depth into rural outlets that took decades to build. Third, a product that fails visibly if substituted — a carpenter who switches adhesive brands risks a job coming apart. This makes trial of alternatives career-risky for the end user.

## The Risks

The primary cost input is **VAM (Vinyl Acetate Monomer)**, an imported petrochemical. VAM prices are volatile — they can move ±50% based on global supply cycles. In FY2022, VAM prices spiked 80%. Pidilite's EBITDA margin compressed from a normalised 21% to 16.4%, and PAT fell 18% year-on-year. The stock fell 35% from its peak over 12 months.

The company typically passes through cost increases over 2–4 quarters with modest pricing actions. This cycle has repeated several times in its history; each time, margins recovered within 12–18 months.

A secondary risk is disruption — new adhesive chemistries, construction methods, or a well-funded competitor executing a sustained price war. None has succeeded in 40 years. That is evidence of durability but not proof of permanence.

## The Valuation Question

An investor analysing Pidilite in mid-2023 faces a genuine dilemma. The business quality is clear. The question is price. At 64× earnings and 79× FCF, the stock embeds aggressive assumptions about future growth. An investor who paid 40× earnings in 2012 — when the stock felt equally "expensive" — earned approximately **18% CAGR** over the next decade as revenue tripled and FCF compounded.

The question is whether 2023 is more like 2012 (expensive but justified) or whether the market has finally priced in enough of the future that the long-run returns from here are merely average.
questions: Using the financial data provided, explain how you would approach estimating Pidilite's intrinsic value. Which specific numbers from the case matter most, and why? What additional things would you need to believe about the business — beyond the current figures — to form a useful estimate?

Pidilite trades at 64× earnings and 79× free cash flow. A colleague says this valuation offers no margin of safety — "you're paying for 80 years of free cash flow upfront." Another says great businesses always deserve premium multiples. Engage seriously with both positions. Under what specific conditions does the current valuation offer genuine margin of safety? Under what conditions is your colleague right?

An investor bought Pidilite in 2012 at 40× earnings, when the stock felt equally expensive. By 2023, revenue had grown 3× and the investment returned ~18% CAGR. What does this episode reveal about the relationship between time horizon and "expensiveness" in a high-quality business? What specific things would have had to go wrong for this investor to have lost money — and how probable were those outcomes?

In FY2022, VAM prices spiked 80%. Pidilite's margins compressed from 21% to 16.4% and profits fell 18%. The stock dropped 35% from its peak. Apply each of the three frameworks from this module — intrinsic value, margin of safety, time horizon — explicitly and separately to explain what a rational value investor should have thought and done at that specific moment.
